notes:
▸ DIGITOXIN IN HFrEF (DIGIT-HF)

Digitoxin did NOT reduce the composite of HF hospitalization + CV death vs placebo. Unlike digoxin (DIG trial — reduced HF hospitalization but not mortality), digitoxin showed no benefit. Digitoxin differs pharmacologically: hepatic metabolism, longer T½ (5-7 days), no renal adjustment needed. Exam trap: do not confuse digitoxin with digoxin results.

▸ cfDNA BLOOD TEST FOR CRC (ECLIPSE)

Shield test: cell-free DNA blood test for CRC screening. Sensitivity 83% for CRC, specificity 90%. Critical limitation: only ~13% sensitivity for advanced adenomas. FDA-approved 2024 as complement (not replacement) for colonoscopy. For patients who decline colonoscopy.

▸ ANTIBIOTIC DURATION FOR BSI (BALANCE)

7 days non-inferior to 14 days for uncomplicated bloodstream infections. 90-day mortality equivalent. CRITICAL EXCLUSIONS: S. aureus (always ≥4 weeks), endocarditis, undrained abscess, prosthetic device infection, severe immunosuppression. Most tested organisms: E. coli, Klebsiella.

▸ ASPIRIN + OAC IN CHRONIC CORONARY SYNDROME

Patients >1yr post-PCI on OAC for AFib: aspirin adds bleeding risk without ischemic benefit. OAC monotherapy preferred. Aligns with AFIRE trial findings.

▸ BAXDROSTAT FOR RESISTANT HTN

Novel aldosterone synthase inhibitor (CYP11B2). Phase 3: ~10-12 mmHg SBP reduction vs placebo. Key side effect: hyperkalemia. First-in-class mechanism. Different from spironolactone/eplerenone (MR antagonists) — blocks aldosterone production upstream.

▸ APIXABAN EXTENDED VTE TREATMENT

Even provoked VTE benefits from extended apixaban 2.5mg BID after initial 6 months. ~80% recurrence reduction vs placebo. Low bleeding risk at reduced dose.

▸ UPADACITINIB FOR GCA (SELECT-GCA)

JAK inhibitor achieved ~60% sustained remission vs ~26% placebo at week 52. Enables faster steroid tapering. JAK class warnings: VTE, infections, malignancy monitoring. Tocilizumab remains standard steroid-sparing agent.

▸ SARCOIDOSIS: PREDNISONE VS MTX (FIRE)

Methotrexate non-inferior to prednisone as FIRST-LINE for pulmonary sarcoidosis. MTX had fewer metabolic side effects. Similar FVC improvement at 24 weeks. May change practice: MTX as first-line rather than steroid-sparing.

▸ SOTATERCEPT FOR PAH (STELLAR)

First-in-class activin signaling inhibitor (TGF-β superfamily trap). Added to background therapy: improved 6MWD, NT-proBNP, WHO functional class. Subcutaneous q3 weeks. Targets disease pathobiology beyond vasodilation.

▸ ECST-2: CAROTID STENOSIS

Asymptomatic or low-risk symptomatic carotid stenosis: optimized medical therapy alone was as good as medical + revascularization at 2 years. Modern medical therapy (high-dose statin, BP control, antiplatelets) has dramatically reduced stroke risk.

▸ STEMI MANAGEMENT

Door-to-balloon <90min for primary PCI, door-to-needle <30min for fibrinolysis if PCI unavailable within 120min. Immediate: aspirin 325mg + P2Y12 inhibitor (ticagrelor or prasugrel preferred over clopidogrel) + heparin + PCI. Post-STEMI: dual antiplatelet 12 months, high-dose statin, beta-blocker, ACEI/ARB, spironolactone if EF≤40%.

▸ NSTEMI RISK STRATIFICATION

TIMI score: age≥65, ≥3 CAD risk factors, known CAD, aspirin use, ≥2 anginal episodes in 24h, ST deviation, elevated troponin. GRACE score for mortality prediction. High-risk features → early invasive strategy (<24h): ongoing ischemia, hemodynamic instability, sustained VT, elevated troponin.

▸ CHRONIC CORONARY SYNDROME

Optimal medical therapy: antiplatelet (aspirin or clopidogrel), high-dose statin (atorvastatin 80mg), BP control, beta-blocker or CCB for angina. Revascularization for: refractory symptoms despite OMT, left main >50%, 3-vessel disease with LV dysfunction, proximal LAD.

▸ ANTIPLATELET MANAGEMENT

Triple therapy (DOAC + aspirin + P2Y12) only during high thrombotic risk period, then drop aspirin early. AFib + PCI: DOAC + P2Y12 for 12 months, then DOAC alone. Never use prasugrel with prior stroke/TIA.

▸ HFrEF GUIDELINE-DIRECTED MEDICAL THERAPY (GDMT)

4 pillars (all reduce mortality): 1) ACEI/ARB/ARNI (sacubitril-valsartan preferred). 2) Beta-blocker (carvedilol, metoprolol succinate, bisoprolol — NOT metoprolol tartrate). 3) MRA (spironolactone or eplerenone). 4) SGLT2i (dapagliflozin or empagliflozin — regardless of diabetes). Add hydralazine-isosorbide dinitrate in Black patients or if ACEI/ARB intolerant.

▸ HFpEF

SGLT2 inhibitors: ONLY drug class with clear outcome benefit (EMPEROR-Preserved, DELIVER). Diuretics for congestion. Treat comorbidities: HTN, AFib, obesity, CAD. Spironolactone: TOPCAT showed possible benefit in Americas subgroup.

▸ ACUTE DECOMPENSATED HF

IV loop diuretics (furosemide). If diuretic resistance: add metolazone or chlorothiazide. Vasodilators (nitroprusside, nitroglycerin) if HTN. Inotropes (dobutamine, milrinone) only for cardiogenic shock — increase mortality in non-shock patients. Do NOT stop beta-blockers unless cardiogenic shock.

▸ DIGITOXIN VS DIGOXIN

Digoxin (DIG trial): reduces HF hospitalization, NOT mortality. Narrow therapeutic window. Digitoxin (DIGIT-HF 2025): NO benefit over placebo — this is a new required article finding.

▸ HYPONATREMIA

Algorithm: 1) Serum osm (exclude pseudohyponatremia). 2) Urine osm (<100 = polydipsia). 3) Volume status → Hypovolemic (UNa<20 extrarenal, >20 renal). Euvolemic: SIADH (UNa>40, Uosm>100), hypothyroid, adrenal insufficiency. Hypervolemic: CHF, cirrhosis, nephrotic.

SIADH diagnosis: euvolemia + hypo-osmolar + inappropriately concentrated urine + UNa>40 + normal thyroid/adrenal. Treatment: fluid restriction. If severe (<120 or symptomatic): hypertonic 3% NaCl. Correction limit: ≤8 mEq/L per 24h (risk of osmotic demyelination syndrome).

▸ HYPERKALEMIA

ECG changes: peaked T → prolonged PR → widened QRS → sine wave → asystole. Treatment ladder: 1) Calcium gluconate (membrane stabilizer, works in 1-3min). 2) Insulin+glucose (shifts K⁺ intracellularly). 3) Sodium bicarbonate (if acidotic). 4) Kayexalate/patiromer/SZC (remove K⁺). 5) Dialysis for refractory.

▸ METABOLIC ACIDOSIS

Anion gap = Na - (Cl + HCO₃). Normal 8-12. High AG: MUDPILES (Methanol, Uremia, DKA, Propylene glycol, INH/Iron, Lactic acidosis, Ethylene glycol, Salicylates). Normal AG (hyperchloremic): diarrhea, RTA, normal saline excess. Delta-delta ratio: ΔAG/ΔHCO₃ — if >2 concurrent metabolic alkalosis, if <1 concurrent non-AG acidosis.

▸ EUGLYCEMIC DKA

SGLT2 inhibitor-associated. Glucose may be normal or mildly elevated (<250). Still has AG metabolic acidosis + ketonemia. Triggered by illness, surgery, dehydration, reduced carb intake. Treatment: stop SGLT2i, IV fluids with dextrose, insulin. Exam pearl from required articles.

▸ SEPSIS (Sepsis-3)

Definition: organ dysfunction (SOFA ≥2) from dysregulated response to infection. qSOFA screening: ≥2 of RR≥22, altered mentation, SBP≤100. Septic shock: vasopressors needed for MAP≥65 + lactate>2 despite adequate resuscitation.

Hour-1 bundle: blood cultures before abx, broad-spectrum antibiotics, 30mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension, vasopressors if fluid-refractory, lactate measurement. First-line vasopressor: norepinephrine.

▸ BSI DURATION (BALANCE TRIAL)

7 days non-inferior to 14 days for uncomplicated BSI. EXCLUDE: S. aureus (always ≥4 weeks), endocarditis, undrained abscess, prosthetic devices.

▸ INFECTIVE ENDOCARDITIS

Duke criteria: 2 major, or 1 major + 3 minor, or 5 minor. Typical organisms: S. viridans (native valve), S. aureus (IVDU — tricuspid; acute — any valve), Enterococcus (elderly/GU procedures), HACEK (culture-negative). Native valve: 4-6 weeks. Prosthetic: 6 weeks minimum.

Surgical indications: HF from valve dysfunction, uncontrolled infection (abscess, persistent bacteremia >7d), large vegetation (>10mm with embolic event), fungal endocarditis.

▸ COMMUNITY-ACQUIRED PNEUMONIA

Outpatient healthy: amoxicillin OR doxycycline OR macrolide. Outpatient with comorbidities: amoxicillin-clavulanate + macrolide, OR respiratory fluoroquinolone. Inpatient non-ICU: beta-lactam + macrolide. ICU: beta-lactam + macrolide (or FQ). Add MRSA coverage if risk factors.

▸ HEALTHCARE-ASSOCIATED INFECTIONS

Catheter-associated UTI: remove/replace catheter + antibiotics. CLABSI: remove line + antibiotics (keep line only for CoNS with short-course). C. diff: oral vancomycin (first-line), fidaxomicin (lower recurrence). Fulminant C. diff: oral vancomycin + IV metronidazole ± rectal vancomycin.

▸ AKI — KDIGO STAGING

Stage 1: Cr ↑1.5-1.9× or ↑≥0.3 in 48h. Stage 2: Cr ↑2.0-2.9×. Stage 3: Cr ↑3× or Cr≥4.0 or RRT initiation. Pre-renal (most common): FENa<1%, BUN/Cr>20, UNa<20, concentrated urine. Intrinsic renal: ATN (FENa>2%, muddy brown casts), AIN (eosinophils, WBC casts), GN (RBC casts, proteinuria). Post-renal: hydronephrosis on US.

▸ CONTRAST-ASSOCIATED AKI

Risk factors: CKD (eGFR<30 highest risk), diabetes, CHF, volume depletion, high contrast volume. Prevention: IV NS hydration pre/post procedure. NAC — no proven benefit (ACT trial). Hold metformin day of and 48h after. SGLT2i: hold 3 days before.

▸ CKD MANAGEMENT

Slowing progression: ACEI/ARB (proteinuria reduction), SGLT2i (DAPA-CKD — benefit even without DM), BP<130/80, glycemic control. Complications by stage: Stage 3: anemia (EPO), hyperphosphatemia (phosphate binders). Stage 4: hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis (bicarb supplementation), secondary hyperPTH (calcitriol/cinacalcet). Stage 5: dialysis planning when eGFR<10-15.

▸ TRIPLE WHAMMY

NSAID + ACEI/ARB + diuretic = acute kidney injury. All three compromise renal autoregulation simultaneously. SGLT2i can act as 4th offender. Exam favorite: elderly patient on all three presenting with AKI after dehydration.

▸ PULMONARY EMBOLISM

Wells score >4: PE likely → CTPA. Wells ≤4: D-dimer → if negative, PE excluded. Massive PE (hemodynamic instability): systemic thrombolysis (alteplase) or catheter-directed therapy. Submassive PE (RV dysfunction but stable): anticoagulation ± thrombolysis (controversial). Initial anticoagulation: LMWH or DOAC.

▸ VTE TREATMENT DURATION

Provoked (surgery/immobility): 3 months. Unprovoked first episode: ≥3 months then reassess (consider extended if low bleeding risk). Cancer-associated: LMWH or DOAC indefinitely while cancer active. Recurrent unprovoked: indefinite anticoagulation. New article: apixaban 2.5mg BID effective for extended treatment even of provoked VTE.

▸ COPD

GOLD classification by spirometry (FEV1/FVC<0.7 post-bronchodilator) + symptoms + exacerbation history. Group A: PRN bronchodilator. Group B: LAMA or LABA. Group E (frequent exacerbations): LABA+LAMA ± ICS (add ICS if eosinophils>300). Acute exacerbation: short-acting bronchodilators, systemic steroids 5 days (REDUCE trial), antibiotics if purulent sputum. NIV for acute hypercapnic respiratory failure.

▸ PULMONARY ARTERIAL HYPERTENSION

Diagnosis: mPAP>20mmHg by RHC, PAWP≤15. Treatment: CCB only if vasoreactive (~5%). ERA + PDE5i or sGC stimulator. Prostacyclin for WHO FC III-IV. NEW: sotatercept (activin trap) — STELLAR trial showed benefit on top of background therapy.

▸ RHEUMATOID ARTHRITIS

Diagnosis: symmetric small joint polyarthritis >6 weeks, RF+, anti-CCP+ (more specific), elevated ESR/CRP, characteristic erosions. Treatment: DMARD early — MTX first-line. If inadequate: add biologic (TNF inhibitor, IL-6 inhibitor, JAK inhibitor, abatacept, rituximab). Cervical spine instability (atlantoaxial subluxation) — check flexion/extension films before intubation.

▸ GIANT CELL ARTERITIS

Age>50, new headache, jaw claudication, visual symptoms, PMR. ESR typically>50 (often>100). Temporal artery biopsy: gold standard (skip lesions — may need bilateral). Treatment: high-dose prednisone 40-60mg immediately (don't wait for biopsy). Steroid-sparing: tocilizumab (FDA-approved), NEW: upadacitinib (SELECT-GCA). Ophthalmologic emergency: anterior ischemic optic neuropathy → blindness if untreated.

▸ ANCA VASCULITIS

GPA (c-ANCA/PR3): upper + lower respiratory + renal, granulomas. MPA (p-ANCA/MPO): renal (RPGN) + pulmonary, no granulomas. EGPA (p-ANCA/MPO ~40%): asthma + eosinophilia + neuropathy. Induction: cyclophosphamide or rituximab + glucocorticoids. Maintenance: rituximab or azathioprine. Plasma exchange for severe renal/pulmonary disease.

▸ GOUT

Acute: NSAIDs, colchicine (within 36h onset), or corticosteroids. Do NOT start/stop allopurinol during acute flare. Chronic: urate-lowering when ≥2 flares/year, tophi, urate nephropathy. Target urate<6mg/dL. Allopurinol (start low, titrate — HLA-B*5801 testing in at-risk populations). Febuxostat: alternative. Pegloticase for refractory.

▸ Atrial Fibrillation
Most common sustained arrhythmia. Rate vs rhythm control — AFFIRM: no mortality benefit for rhythm control in older patients. Rate control target <110 bpm (RACE-II). CHA₂DS₂-VASc for stroke risk: ≥2 in men or ≥3 in women → anticoagulation. DOACs preferred over warfarin (RE-LY, ROCKET-AF, ARISTOTLE). Cardioversion: TEE or 3 weeks anticoag + 4 weeks post. Amiodarone most effective but toxicity (thyroid, pulmonary, hepatic, corneal). Dronedarone: not in HFrEF or permanent AF.

▸ Supraventricular Tachycardia
AVNRT most common regular SVT. Vagal maneuvers → IV adenosine 6mg→12mg. WPW: wide-complex with delta wave — AVOID AV nodal blockers (digoxin, verapamil, adenosine). Procainamide or cardioversion for WPW with AF.

▸ Ventricular Arrhythmias
Sustained VT >30s: hemodynamically stable → amiodarone or procainamide. Unstable → synchronized cardioversion. VF/pulseless VT → defibrillation + epinephrine + amiodarone. Torsades de Pointes: IV magnesium + overdrive pacing. QTc prolongation: >500ms high risk. Common culprits: antiarrhythmics, fluoroquinolones, azithromycin, antipsychotics, ondansetron.

▸ Bradyarrhythmias
First-degree AV block: benign. Mobitz I (Wenckebach): usually nodal, often benign. Mobitz II: infra-nodal, high risk of progression — pacemaker. Third-degree: complete dissociation — emergent pacing. Symptomatic sinus bradycardia → atropine 0.5mg IV.

▸ Aortic Stenosis
Most common valvular disease requiring intervention. Causes: calcific/degenerative (>70y), bicuspid (50-70y), rheumatic. Classic triad: angina, syncope, HF. Severe: valve area <1.0 cm², mean gradient >40 mmHg, Vmax >4 m/s. Medical therapy does NOT alter progression. Indications for AVR: symptomatic severe AS, asymptomatic with LVEF <50%, undergoing other cardiac surgery. TAVR for high/prohibitive surgical risk (PARTNER trials).

▸ Mitral Regurgitation
Acute: chordae rupture, papillary muscle dysfunction (MI), endocarditis → pulmonary edema, cardiogenic shock. Chronic: volume overload → LA/LV dilation. Primary (organic): valve pathology. Secondary (functional): LV dilation. Surgery when: symptomatic, LVEF ≤60%, LVESD ≥40mm.

▸ Mitral Stenosis
Almost always rheumatic. Progressive dyspnea, orthopnea, hemoptysis. AF common (LA dilation). Loud S1 + opening snap + low-pitched diastolic rumble. Severe: valve area <1.5 cm². Anticoagulation for AF. Percutaneous mitral balloon valvotomy if favorable anatomy.

▸ Aortic Regurgitation
Acute: endocarditis, aortic dissection → severe pulmonary edema. Chronic: widened pulse pressure, bounding pulses (Corrigan's), de Musset's sign. Surgery when symptomatic or LVEF <55% or LVESD >50mm.

▸ Definitions & Targets
Stage 1: 130-139/80-89. Stage 2: ≥140/90. Hypertensive urgency: >180/120 without end-organ damage. Emergency: >180/120 WITH end-organ damage (encephalopathy, AKI, aortic dissection, pulmonary edema). Target <130/80 for most adults (SPRINT trial). Elderly ≥75y: systolic <130 still beneficial.

▸ Secondary Causes (screen if resistant, young, or sudden onset)
Renal artery stenosis (fibromuscular dysplasia in young women, atherosclerotic in older), primary aldosteronism (most common — ARR >30), pheochromocytoma (episodic HTN, headache, sweating, tachycardia — 24h urine metanephrines), Cushing's, coarctation (BP differential arms/legs), OSA, thyroid disease.

▸ First-Line Agents
ACEi/ARB: preferred with DM, proteinuria, HF. Never combine ACEi+ARB. Contraindicated in bilateral RAS, pregnancy, hyperkalemia. CCB (amlodipine): preferred in Black patients, elderly. Thiazide: chlorthalidone preferred over HCTZ. Beta-blocker: NOT first-line unless compelling indication (HF, post-MI, rate control).

▸ Resistant HTN
≥3 drugs including diuretic at optimal doses. Confirm adherence, exclude white-coat effect (ABPM). Add spironolactone (PATHWAY-2 trial). Consider Baxdrostat (aldosterone synthase inhibitor) — P0064 article.

▸ Hypertensive Emergency
IV labetalol, nicardipine, or nitroprusside. Reduce MAP by 25% in first hour. Aortic dissection: target SBP <120 + HR <60 — IV esmolol + nitroprusside.

▸ GI Bleeding
Upper (above ligament of Treitz): hematemesis, melena. Causes: PUD (most common), varices, Mallory-Weiss. Glasgow-Blatchford score for risk. PPI IV drip + endoscopy within 24h. Variceal: octreotide + ceftriaxone + emergent endoscopy (band ligation). Lower: hematochezia. Causes: diverticulosis (most common), angiodysplasia, colorectal cancer. CTA if hemodynamically significant.

▸ Cirrhosis
Child-Pugh: A (5-6), B (7-9), C (10-15). MELD: bilirubin + creatinine + INR — transplant listing. Complications: ascites (2g Na restriction + spironolactone ± furosemide, paracentesis if tense, SBP prophylaxis with norfloxacin if prior SBP or protein <1.5), SBP (PMN >250 → empiric ceftriaxone + albumin), hepatorenal syndrome (type 1 rapidly progressive — terlipressin + albumin), hepatic encephalopathy (lactulose + rifaximin), variceal bleeding.

▸ Acute Liver Failure
Coagulopathy (INR >1.5) + encephalopathy + no prior liver disease. Causes: acetaminophen (most common in West), viral hepatitis, autoimmune, Wilson's. N-acetylcysteine for acetaminophen (and possibly non-acetaminophen ALF). King's College Criteria for transplant.

▸ Jaundice
Unconjugated: hemolysis, Gilbert's (benign, <5 mg/dL), ineffective erythropoiesis. Conjugated: hepatocellular (elevated ALT/AST) vs cholestatic (elevated ALP/GGT). Obstructive: dilated ducts on US → MRCP/ERCP.

▸ DKA vs HHS
DKA: T1DM or late T2DM. Glucose >250, pH <7.3, bicarb <18, anion gap >12, ketones+. Treatment: NS bolus → 0.1 U/kg/hr insulin → when glucose <200 switch to D5 + reduce insulin → close gap before stopping. HHS: T2DM, glucose >600, osmolality >320, minimal ketosis. Treatment: aggressive fluid resuscitation (often 6-9L deficit).

▸ Diabetes Management
HbA1c target <7% for most. Metformin: first-line, avoid if eGFR <30. SGLT2i (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin): CV and renal benefit, risk of euglycemic DKA, Fournier's gangrene. GLP-1 RA (semaglutide, liraglutide): weight loss, CV benefit. Insulin: basal-bolus for T1DM; basal ± prandial for T2DM when oral agents fail.

▸ Thyroid
Hyperthyroidism: Graves' (TSI+, diffuse uptake), toxic multinodular goiter, toxic adenoma. Treatment: methimazole (preferred) or PTU (pregnancy 1st trimester). Thyroid storm: PTU + iodine (1hr after PTU) + propranolol + hydrocortisone. Hypothyroidism: Hashimoto's most common. Levothyroxine, check TSH 6-8 weeks after dose change. Myxedema coma: IV levothyroxine + hydrocortisone.

▸ Adrenal
Addison's (primary insufficiency): hypotension, hyperpigmentation, hyperkalemia, hyponatremia. Cosyntropin stimulation test. Treatment: hydrocortisone + fludrocortisone. Adrenal crisis: IV hydrocortisone 100mg bolus + fluids. Cushing's: 24h urine cortisol, overnight dexamethasone suppression test, late-night salivary cortisol.

▸ Pheochromocytoma
Rule of 10s: 10% bilateral, 10% malignant, 10% extra-adrenal. 24h urine metanephrines or plasma free metanephrines. Alpha-blockade BEFORE beta-blockade (phenoxybenzamine → propranolol). Surgery after adequate blockade.

▸ Anemia Workup
MCV-based: Microcytic (<80): iron deficiency (low ferritin, high TIBC), thalassemia (target cells, normal/high ferritin), ACD, sideroblastic. Normocytic (80-100): ACD, renal (low EPO), hemolysis (high LDH, low haptoglobin, high retic), aplastic. Macrocytic (>100): B12/folate deficiency (hypersegmented neutrophils), MDS, liver disease, hypothyroidism, drugs (methotrexate, hydroxyurea).

▸ Iron Deficiency
Most common anemia worldwide. Low ferritin (<30) is diagnostic. If ferritin 30-100 with inflammation, check TSAT (<20% supports IDA). Oral iron: ferrous sulfate 325mg (65mg elemental) on empty stomach with vitamin C. IV iron if oral intolerance, CKD, IBD, or severe deficiency.

▸ Hemolysis
Intravascular: mechanical (DIC, TTP, HUS, prosthetic valve), PNH, complement-mediated. Extravascular: autoimmune (warm IgG — spleen; cold IgM — liver), hereditary spherocytosis, G6PD. Labs: elevated LDH + indirect bilirubin, low haptoglobin, reticulocytosis. Direct Coombs (DAT) positive in autoimmune.

▸ Coagulation
PT/INR: extrinsic pathway (VII) — warfarin monitoring. PTT: intrinsic pathway (VIII, IX, XI, XII) — heparin monitoring. Mixing study: corrects = factor deficiency; doesn't correct = inhibitor (e.g., lupus anticoagulant). DIC: low platelets + low fibrinogen + high D-dimer + prolonged PT/PTT + schistocytes.

▸ Anticoagulation
DOACs: dabigatran (thrombin inhibitor, renal clearance, idarucizumab reversal), rivaroxaban/apixaban/edoxaban (Xa inhibitors, andexanet alfa reversal). Warfarin: vitamin K antagonist, CYP2C9 metabolism, INR target 2-3. HIT: platelet drop >50% 5-10 days after heparin, serotonin release assay or anti-PF4. Switch to argatroban or bivalirudin.

▸ Ischemic Stroke
Time is brain. NIHSS for severity. CT head (rule out hemorrhage) → tPA if <4.5h from onset (NINDS, ECASS-III). Thrombectomy for large vessel occlusion up to 24h (DAWN, DEFUSE-3). Aspirin within 24-48h. Dual antiplatelet (aspirin+clopidogrel) for 21 days in minor stroke/TIA (CHANCE, POINT). Secondary prevention: statin, antihypertensive, anticoagulation for AF source. Carotid endarterectomy for symptomatic >70% stenosis (NASCET). ECST-2 article in syllabus.

▸ Intracerebral Hemorrhage
Hypertensive (basal ganglia, thalamus, pons, cerebellum). Other: amyloid angiopathy (lobar, elderly), AVM, coagulopathy. Acute: reverse anticoagulation, SBP target <140 (INTERACT2, ATACH-2). Cerebellar hemorrhage >3cm → surgical evacuation. IVH with hydrocephalus → EVD.

▸ Seizures
New-onset: structural (tumor, stroke, TBI), metabolic (hyponatremia, hypoglycemia, uremia), drug withdrawal. Status epilepticus: >5 min continuous or ≥2 without recovery. Treatment: benzodiazepine (lorazepam 4mg IV) → levetiracetam or fosphenytoin → if refractory, propofol/midazolam drip + intubation. First unprovoked seizure: 40% recurrence risk, may defer AEDs. Two seizures → start AEDs.

▸ GBS
Ascending symmetric weakness, areflexia, albuminocytologic dissociation in CSF (high protein, normal cells). NCS: demyelinating pattern. Monitor FVC — intubate if <20 mL/kg or declining. Treatment: IVIG or plasmapheresis (equally effective). Do NOT use steroids.

▸ Myasthenia Gravis
Fluctuating weakness, worse with activity. Ocular (ptosis, diplopia) → generalized. AChR antibodies (85%), MuSK antibodies (5-10%). Edrophonium (Tensilon) test. CT chest for thymoma. Treatment: pyridostigmine, immunosuppression (prednisone, azathioprine). Crisis: IVIG or PLEX. Avoid aminoglycosides, beta-blockers, magnesium.

▸ Shock Classification
Distributive (most common): septic, anaphylactic, neurogenic. Low SVR, high CO. Cardiogenic: MI, myocarditis, valvular catastrophe. Low CO, high SVR, high PCWP. Hypovolemic: hemorrhage, dehydration. Low CO, high SVR, low PCWP. Obstructive: PE, tamponade, tension pneumothorax.

▸ Sepsis (Sepsis-3)
SIRS criteria abandoned. Sepsis: suspected infection + SOFA ≥2 (or qSOFA ≥2 for screening). Septic shock: sepsis + vasopressor requirement + lactate >2 mmol/L despite adequate fluid. Hour-1 bundle: lactate, blood cultures, broad-spectrum antibiotics, 30 mL/kg crystalloid if hypotensive/lactate ≥4, vasopressors for MAP ≥65 after fluids.

▸ Vasopressors
Norepinephrine: first-line for septic shock (α1 > β1). Vasopressin: add if NE 0.25-0.5 mcg/kg/min needed (VASST trial). Epinephrine: add or substitute if refractory. Dobutamine: add for low CO states. Phenylephrine: pure α1, for neurogenic shock or tachyarrhythmia-limiting NE use.

▸ ARDS
Berlin criteria: acute onset (<1 week), bilateral opacities on CXR, not fully explained by HF/fluid overload, P/F ratio: mild 200-300, moderate 100-200, severe <100. Management: low tidal volume ventilation (6 mL/kg IBW, ARDSNet), plateau pressure <30, prone positioning if P/F <150 (PROSEVA), conservative fluid strategy, neuromuscular blockade if P/F <150 (ACURASYS).

▸ Cardiac Arrest
Shockable (VF/pulseless VT): defibrillation + CPR + epinephrine q3-5min + amiodarone 300mg→150mg. Non-shockable (PEA/asystole): CPR + epinephrine. H's and T's: hypovolemia, hypoxia, hydrogen ion (acidosis), hypo/hyperkalemia, hypothermia; tension pneumothorax, tamponade, toxins, thrombosis (PE/MI). Post-arrest: targeted temperature management 32-36°C for 24h.

▸ Tumor Lysis Syndrome
Rapid cell death → hyperuricemia, hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, hypocalcemia. Risk: high tumor burden (Burkitt's, ALL, large cell lymphoma). Prevention: aggressive hydration + allopurinol or rasburicase (preferred in high risk). Treatment: rasburicase, dialysis if refractory.

▸ Hypercalcemia of Malignancy
Mechanisms: PTHrP (squamous cell, renal), osteolytic metastases (breast, myeloma), calcitriol (lymphoma). Treatment: NS hydration (200-500 mL/hr) + zoledronic acid (onset 48-72h). Calcitonin for bridge (rapid but tachyphylaxis). Denosumab if refractory.

▸ Superior Vena Cava Syndrome
Facial/upper extremity edema, JVD, dyspnea. Causes: lung cancer (most common), lymphoma, thrombus from central line. CT with contrast for diagnosis. Treatment: radiation + chemo (SCLC, lymphoma) or stent (NSCLC, thrombus).

▸ Febrile Neutropenia
ANC <500 + fever ≥38.3°C (or ≥38°C sustained >1h). MASCC score for risk stratification. High risk: IV piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime, or meropenem. Add vancomycin if: catheter infection, skin/soft tissue, hemodynamic instability, MRSA risk. Add antifungal if persistent fever >4-7 days.

▸ Spinal Cord Compression
Back pain (95%), motor weakness, sensory level, bladder dysfunction (late). MRI whole spine urgently. Dexamethasone 10mg IV bolus then 4mg q6h. Radiation ± surgery (unstable spine, unknown histology, single level).

▸ Cardiac Risk Assessment
Revised Cardiac Risk Index (RCRI/Lee): 6 predictors — ischemic heart disease, HF, cerebrovascular disease, insulin-dependent DM, creatinine >2, high-risk surgery. 0 points: <1% risk. ≥3 points: >5% risk. Functional capacity: ≥4 METs (climb stairs, walk uphill) → proceed. Poor functional capacity + elevated RCRI → consider stress testing.

▸ Medication Management
Continue: beta-blockers (do NOT start new preop), statins, most antihypertensives. Hold: ACEi/ARB morning of surgery (hypotension risk), metformin (lactic acidosis, restart when renal function confirmed), SGLT2i (euglycemic DKA), DOACs (2-3 days pre-op, 5 days for dabigatran if CrCl <50). Bridging anticoagulation: only for mechanical mitral valve, recent VTE (<3mo), high-risk AF (CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥7).

▸ VTE Prophylaxis
All surgical patients: risk-stratify with Caprini score. Low risk: early ambulation. Moderate/high: LMWH or UFH. Orthopedic: extended prophylaxis 35 days (LMWH, rivaroxaban, or aspirin for total hip/knee). Contraindication to pharmacologic: IPC devices.

▸ Acetaminophen Overdose
Most common cause of acute liver failure. Toxic dose >150 mg/kg. Rumack-Matthew nomogram (4-24h post-ingestion). Treatment: N-acetylcysteine (NAC) — 72h oral or 21h IV protocol. Start empirically if presentation >8h even before level. King's College Criteria for transplant.

▸ Toxidromes
Anticholinergic: hot, dry, red, blind, mad — atropine-like drugs. Treatment: physostigmine. Cholinergic (SLUDGE): salivation, lacrimation, urination, diarrhea, GI cramps, emesis. Organophosphates. Treatment: atropine + pralidoxime. Sympathomimetic: hypertension, tachycardia, hyperthermia, diaphoresis, mydriasis. Cocaine, amphetamines. Treatment: benzodiazepines. Opioid: miosis, respiratory depression, CNS depression. Treatment: naloxone.

▸ Specific Antidotes
Benzodiazepines → flumazenil (caution in chronic use — seizures). Methanol/ethylene glycol → fomepizole (ADH inhibitor) + dialysis. Digoxin → digoxin-specific Fab. Iron → deferoxamine. Cyanide → hydroxocobalamin. Beta-blocker → glucagon. CCB → high-dose insulin + calcium. Isoniazid → pyridoxine (B6). Warfarin → vitamin K + PCC/FFP.

▸ Alcohol Withdrawal
Onset 6-24h after last drink. Timeline: tremor/anxiety (6-12h), seizures (12-48h), delirium tremens (48-96h — 5% mortality). CIWA-Ar protocol: symptom-triggered benzodiazepines (diazepam or chlordiazepoxide). Thiamine before glucose. Refractory: phenobarbital or propofol.
